texts.label: text
texts.orig: A physicochemical model developed in earlier work for representing H2S and C02 solubility in aqueous solutions of monoethanolamine (MEA) and diethanolamine (DEA) was extended to include the mixtures of methyldiethanolamine (MDEA) with MEA or DEA. The framework of the model is based upon both liquid-phase chemical equilibria and vapor-liquid (phase) equilibria. Activity coefficients are represented with the electrolyte-NRTL equation treating both long-range electrostatic interactions and short-range binary interactions. Adjustable binary interaction parameters of the model were fitted on binary and ternary system MDEA data reported in the literature. The solubility of C02 in aqueous mixtures of MDEA with MEA or DEA was measured at 40 and 80 °C over a wide range of C02 partial pressures. Representation of the data by the model is good, especially at low to moderate acid gas loadings.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Design of gas-treating processes with MDEA-based aqueous solvents requires vapor-liquid equilibrium (VLE) data for the corresponding acid gas-alkanolamine-water systems. Several experimental studies of the VLE behavior of acid gas-MDEA-water systems have been published in the literature (Jou et al., 1982,1986, Bhairi, 1984). Representation of experimental data with a thermodynamically rigorous model is needed so that one can confidently and systematically interpolate and extrapolate. No measurements of C02 solubility in alkanolamine mixtures have, as yet, been reported. However, as the number of components, and thus the degrees of freedom, in a system increases, representation of data becomes more important. Chakravaraty (1985) used the model of Deshmukh and Mather (1981) to represent H2S and C02 solubility in aqueous solutions of MDEA and blends of MDEA with MEA and DEA. Activity coefficients were represented with Guggenheim's equation (Guggenheim, 1935). However, this equation for activity coefficients is valid only to ionic strengths approaching 0.1 mol kg"1 (Pitzer, 1973).

Chakravaraty (1985) used the model of Deshmukh and Mather (1981) to represent H2S and C02 solubility in aqueous solutions of MDEA and blends of MDEA with MEA and DEA. texts.label: text
texts.orig: Recent research has led to several semiempirical excess Gibbs energy models and/or activity coefficients models for aqueous electrolyte systems valid to ionic strengths representative of industrial applications. Among them are the models of Chen and co-workers (Chen et al., 1982; Chen and Evans, 1986; Mock et al., 1986; Scauflaire et al., 1989) combining Debye-Hückel theory to account for long-range electrostatic interactions between ions and NRTL local composition theory (Renon and Prausnitz, 1968) to account for short-range van der Waals type interactions between all liquid-phase species, molecular and ionic. Recently, we used Chen's electrolyte-NRTL equation in the context of a VLE model to represent activity coefficients of all species, ionic and molecular, in acid gas-MEA-water and acid gas-DEA-water systems (Austgen et al., 1989).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The objective of this work was to extend the physicochemical VLE model developed in our previous research to represent H2S and C02 solubility in aqueous solutions of MDEA and C02 solubility in mixtures of MDEA with MEA and DEA over the temperature range from 25 to 120 6C and amine concentration range from 1.0 to 4.3 kmol m"3. Adjustable parameters of the model, binary energy interaction parameters, were fitted on experimental binary and ternary system VLE data reported in the literature and/or measured in this work.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The experimental apparatus is shown in Figure 1. This was a continuous flow apparatus. The system consisted of two 300-mL stainless steel cylinders mounted vertically in a thermostated water bath. Temperature was controlled to within ±0.2 °C at 40 °C and ±0.4 °C at 80 °C. The first cylinder served as a water saturator. The second cylinder served as the equilibrium cell containing the alkanolamine solution. Inserted through the top of each cell was 0.25-in. stainless steel tube around which a gas-tight seal was made with compression fittings. Inside each cylinder a no. 12C gas dispersion tube was connected to the stainless tube. The dispersion tubes extended to the bottom of each cell, approximately 13 cm below the liquid surface.
texts.text: The experimental apparatus is shown in Figure 1. This was a continuous flow apparatus. The system consisted of two 300-mL stainless steel cylinders mounted vertically in a thermostated water bath. Temperature was controlled to within ±0.2 °C at 40 °C and ±0.4 °C at 80 °C. The first cylinder served as a water saturator. The second cylinder served as the equilibrium cell containing the alkanolamine solution. Inserted through the top of each cell was 0.25-in. stainless steel tube around which a gas-tight seal was made with compression fittings. Inside each cylinder a no. 12C gas dispersion tube was connected to the stainless tube. The dispersion tubes extended to the bottom of each cell, approximately 13 cm below the liquid surface.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Precisely calibrated Brooks Mass Flow Controllers, Series 5850, powered and controlled by a Brooks Controller, Model 5878, were used to deliver N2 and C02/N2 mixtures to the equilibrium apparatus. Total flow rate was maintained at 60 to 100 mL min"1. The effluent gas from the equilibrium cell passed through a condenser operating near 0 °C to remove water and then to either a Horiba infared C02 analyzer, Model PIR-2000, (for C02 concentrations below 1%) or an Infared Industries, Inc., C02 analyzer, Model IR702 (for C02 concentrations to 10%). The analyzers were calibrated daily with the appropriate C02/N2 gas mixtures.

texts.orig: As shown in Figure 1, the feed gas could be redirected and delivered directly to one of the infared C02 analyzers. During a measurement, the C02 content of the feed gas was adjusted by use of the mass flow controllers until the C02 concentration in the feed gas was identical with the C02 concentration in the effluent from the equilibrium cell. When the feed and effluent concentrations were equal, the C02 partial pressure of the feed gas was taken to be the C02 partial pressure in equilibrium with the alkanolamine solution. The system was operated at total pressures from 100 to 300 kPa.

The (apparent) C02 content of the solution was then measured with the use of an Oceanography International Model 525 carbon analyzer. A sample of the stock NaOH solution was analyzed for C02 content simultaneously with carbonated NaOH samples so that the C02 concentration of the stock NaOH solution could be subtracted from the value measured for the carbonated samples. The liquid analyzer was calibrated with 0.010 and 0.100 M solutions of Na2C03. The liquid analyzer was calibrated with 0.010 and 0.100 M solutions of Na2C03. texts.orig: Activity Coefficients. The electrolyte-NRTL equation (Chen and Evans, 1986), modified for mixed solvent electrolyte solutions (Scauflaire et al., 1989), is used to represent liquid-phase activity coefficients. This is a generalized excess Gibbs energy model that accounts for molecular/ionic interactions between all true liquid-phase species. The basic postulate of the model is that the molar excess Gibbs energy of an electrolyte solution can be written as the sum of two contributions, one related to the short-range or local interactions that exist in the immediate neighborhood of any species and the other related to the long-range (LR) ion-ion interactions that exist beyond the immediate neighborhood of a central ionic species:

texts.orig: The adjustable parameters of the electrolyte-NRTL equation are binary interaction energy parameters, r¡¡, and corresponding nonrandomness factors, a¡¡, of the NRTL contribution to the excess Gibbs energy function. Three types of short-range binary interactions are accounted for by the electrolyte-NRTL equation: molecule-molecule, molecule-ion pair, and ion pair-ion pair (with a common cation or anion). Following the approach adopted in our previous work (Austgen et al., 1989), nonrandomness factors corresponding to molecule-molecule interactions («''-) and water-ion pair interactions (aWiC1 or a^) were fixed at a value of 0.2. Nonrandomness factors for alkanolamine-ion pair and acid gas-ion pair interactions were fixed at 0.1. Therefore, the only adjustable parameters of the model are the short-range binary interaction parameters representing energies of interaction between liquidphase species. A discussion of the physical significance and importance of relative values of these parameters can be found elsewhere (Chen, 1980).

texts.orig: A large number of binary interaction parameters, molecule-molecule, molecule-ion pair, and ion pair-ion pair, can, in principle, be formulated for the acid gas-alkanolamine-water system. However, because many of the ionic species formed by dissociation are present in the liquid phase at low or negligible concentrations, parameters associated with them do not significantly affect representation of VLE. Important parameters in the system were identified by the statistical significance with which they could be fitted on experimental VLE data. These are listed in Table VII.

MEA-water and DEA-water parameters were fitted on binary system VLE (TPx) data and reported in our earlier work (Austgen et al., 1989). texts.orig: Several trends are evident in the water-ion pair and ion pair-water parameters reported in Table VII. In general, water-ion pair and ion pair-water interaction parameters at 298.15 K are in good agreement with default values of 8.0 and -4.0, respectively. Note also that, except for DEA-water interaction parameters, most of parameters are well-defined as indicated by the relatively small values of the associated parameter standard deviations.

texts.orig: The temperature dependence of the adjusted interaction parameters also exhibits interesting trends. In general, the water-ion pair interaction parameters were found to be relatively strong functions of temperature as indicated by the relative contribution of the temperature-dependent term to the corresponding interaction parameters. However, the corresponding ion pair-water interaction parameters were generally found to be weak functions of temperature or independent of temperature. Note also that the water-ion pair interaction parameters which assumed the largest (absolute) values among all adjustable parameters were those for which the anion was carbamate of DEA. The same trends were seen for the corresponding parameters of the MEA ternary systems (Austgen et al., 1989).

texts.orig: It is well-known that the parameters of the NRTL equation are often highly correlated (Renon, 1985). Results of the work conducted by Chen and Evans (1986) and results of this work suggest that corresponding pairs of water-ion pair interaction parameters of the electrolyteNRTL equation are also highly correlated. This is easily seen in corresponding values of the water-ion pair and ion pair-water interaction parameters reported at 298.15 K in Table VII: Water-ion pair interactions parameters are roughly 2 times greater in magnitude than the corresponding ion pair-water interaction parameters.

texts.orig: However, the data regression system of ASPEN provides estimates of the parameter correlation coefficients via the parameter variance-covariance matrix (see Beck and Arnold, 1977) so that parameter correlation may be quantified. In general, the coefficients of eq 10 for any given binary interaction parameter were found to be highly correlated. Moreover, the coefficients of eq 10 for a water-ion pair interaction parameter were found to exhibit moderate to high degrees of correlation with the coefficients for the corresponding ion pair-water interaction parameter. This is consistent with the observation that water-ion pair interaction parameters were estimated to be roughly 2 times greater in magnitude than the corresponding ion pair-water interaction parameters.

texts.orig: Carbamate Stability Constants. Because of the lack of reliable experimental data for the carbamate stability constant (reaction 7a) for MEA and DEA, values for these equilibrium constants were fitted simultaneously with binary interaction parameters on C02-amine-H20 ternary system VLE data and reported previously (Austgen et al., 1989). In view of the new data for DEA-water systems that became available to us, the carbamate stability constant (coefficients of eq 6) was reestimated simultaneously with binary interaction parameters on the C02-DEA-H20 VLE data used in the previous work. The new value is reported as a function of temperature in Table V.
texts.text: Carbamate Stability Constants. In view of the new data for DEA-water systems that became available to us, the carbamate stability constant (coefficients of eq 6) was reestimated simultaneously with binary interaction parameters on the C02-DEA-H20 VLE data used in the previous work. The new value is reported as a function of temperature in Table V.

texts.orig: The adjusted coefficients of eq 7 for the DEA carbamate stability constant were found to be highly correlated but relatively well determined as measured by the estimated standard deviations of each coefficient (Austgen, 1989). The same coefficients were generally poorly or moderately correlated with the fitted values of the binary interaction parameters. This is fortuitous as it suggests that the chemical interactions are largely being separated from the physical interactions by the data regression process.

texts.orig: Ternary Systems: Experimental Data Representation. Estimated 'true' values of each of the measured state variables are found during the course of parameter estimation using the maximum likelihood method (Britt and Luecke, 1973). Figures 2 and 3 are graphical summaries of the ratios of estimated 'true' to experimentally measured values of equilibrium H2S and C02 partial pressures for the MDEA-H2S-H20 and MDEA-C02-H20 systems, respectively. Figures summarizing the ratios of the estimated 'true' to experimental liquid-phase acid gas concentration show similar behavior and are thus not included here. The relative magnitudes of the ratios of estimated 'true' to measured values are approximately equal for the two state variables. Estimated 'true' values of the system temperature were generally in close agreement with measured values, making it unnecessary to consider similar plots for temperature.

texts.orig: Jou et al. (1986) report a large number of H2S and C02 solubilities in a 35 wt % (approximately 3.04 kmol m~3) MDEA solution. To illustrate data representation for the quaternary system, the ratios of the estimated 'true' to experimentally measured values of the acid gas equilibrium partial pressures and liquid-phase apparent mole fractions for a representative sampling of Jou's data at 40 and 100 °C are presented in Table VIII. Again, if the data were error-free and if the model represented the data perfectly, all ratios of 'true' to measured state variables would be unity. As shown in Table VIII, estimated 'true' values of the state variables are in satisfactory agreement with experimental data at 40 °C except at low acid gas loadings. At 100 °C, agreement between estimated 'true' and experimental concentrations is good, but there is moderate disagreement between estimated 'true' and experimental values of the acid gas partial pressures.

texts.orig: It can be seen in Figures 4 and 5 that the C02 solubility curve for a 2 kmol m'3 MDEA, 2 kmol m"3 MEA mixture approaches the C02 solubility curve for the 4 kmol m"3 MEA mixture at very low loadings. The same is seen to be true in Figures 6 and 7 for the MDEA-DEA mixtures. This behavior is expected. At very low loadings the primary mechanism by which C02 is absorbed is through reaction with promoter (MEA or DEA) to form the carbamate of the promoter. As C02 loading increases in the amine mixtures, the ratio of molecular MDEA to promoter increases. Therefore, because of the law of mass action, both promoter and MDEA are seen to significantly affect the solubility of C02 at any given partial pressure.

texts.orig: The reaction of MDEA with C02 to form bicarbonate results in the conversion of one amine molecule for each molecule of C02 reacted. Hence, for an aqueous solution of MDEA, it can be assumed (as a first-order approximation) that amine will be able to react with C02 to a loading of unity. By this argument, the C02-MDEA equilibrium curve crosses the C02-MEA and C02-DEA equilibrium curves largely because MEA and DEA form stable carbamate species.

texts.orig: At loadings approaching 1 mol of C02/mol of amine, the C02 solubility curve for the amine mixture once again approaches the equilibrium curve for the 4 kmol m"3 MEA or DEA solution. We believe that this behavior is due to the stability of the carbamate of promoter, which is present in the mixed amine solutions even at these high loadings. That this is true will be seen more clearly in the next section when liquid-phase concentrations are examined. It appears that, at moderate to high loadings, the reversion of carbamate of the primary or secondary amine to bicarbonate plays an important, if not controlling, role in determining the solubility of C02.

texts.orig: Figure 12 indicates that DEA disappears at a slower rate with loading than does MEA in the corresponding amine mixtures. This is consistent with the fact that the carbamate of DEA is not as stable as the carbamate of MEA. Furthermore, while DEA disappears faster than MDEA as the C02 loading initially increases from zero, molecular DEA is present in the solution to a loading of 0.9 mol/mol. These results suggest that while the addition of a given amount of MEA will affect C02 solubility greater at low loadings, DEA will enhance the absorption rate of C02 in an MDEA-based solvent mixture to higher loadings.

texts.orig: Also of interest in Figures 11 and 12 are the relative concentrations of MEA or DEA and MDEA. The concentrations of the unreacted alkanolamines are important in determining the rate of absorption in a rate-based simulator and in laboratory measurements of reaction rates. In the MDEA-MEA mixture, the concentration of MDEA is essentially constant at loadings below 0.1 mol/mol while the concentration of MEA decreases rapidly with loading in this range. MEA preferentially reacts because it forms a very stable carbamate and, to a lesser extent, because of the relatively large pKa of MEA. For a 3 kmol m'3 MDEA, 1 kmol m"8 MEA mixture at 40 °C, Figure 12 indicates that MEA is almost completely converted to protonated MEA or carbamate at a loading of 0.4 mol of C02/mol of amine.
